title: Chicken and Andouille Gumbo
subtitle: Louisiana gumbo built on a dark roux with chicken, sausage, and the holy trinity
sourceNotes: Gumbo is Louisiana's state dish, with roots in West African, Native American, and French Creole culinary traditions. The dark roux method is consistent with Louisiana cookery from the eighteenth century. The Picayune's Creole Cook Book (1901) contains several gumbo recipes; Paul Prudhomme's Louisiana Kitchen (1984) is the modern canonical reference.

content.text: is the defining step of gumbo and cannot be rushed or abandoned mid-cook. Commit twenty to thirty minutes of constant stirring before you start anything else, the whole dish depends on the roux being properly dark. Use a heavy pan, medium heat, and do not leave the hob.

Combine {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and {{plain-flour}} of flour in a large heavy casserole over a medium heat. Stir constantly with a wooden spoon for 20 to 30 minutes until dark chocolate coloured. The moment it is done, add the
(onion, celery, and green pepper) all at once. Stir and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and spices for 1 minute.

Ladle in {{chicken-stock}} of hot stock gradually, stirring after each addition. Add the chicken, {{andouille-sausage}} of sausage, {{okra}} of okra, and bay leaves. Simmer uncovered for 45 to 55 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the gumbo has thickened. Remove the chicken, pull the meat from the bones, and return the meat to the pot. Remove the bay leaves, taste, and adjust seasoning. Serve over white rice with spring onions and parsley.
Traditional Cajun gumbo is sometimes finished with

, ground dried sassafras leaves, stirred in off the heat just before serving. It adds a subtle herbal, slightly bitter flavour and thickens the gumbo further. It is not essential but is the traditional finishing touch. Add 1 tsp off the heat if you have it.
